This is **Putu Piring (steamed rice cake, palm sugar)**. Putu Piring is a traditional Malay steamed kueh of lightly fragrant coarse rice flour pressed into small round moulds with a nugget of gula melaka (palm sugar) hidden in the centre, then steamed until the rice flour sets into a soft, slightly granular cake. When bitten, the molten gula melaka filling flows out, imparting a deep, caramel-sweet, slightly smoky palm sugar flavour. The cakes are served on banana leaf with freshly grated coconut and eaten immediately while warm. Putu Piring is associated with traditional Malay kueh stalls and is one of Singapore's most nostalgic street foods. Per 100g: 192.0 kcal, 2.2g protein, 3.5g fat, 39.5g carbohydrates, 1.5g dietary fibre, 65.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 72 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 28.4. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — coarse rice flour, steamed; GI elevated by gelatinised rice starch; gula melaka adds significant sugar. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 65mg per 4-piece serving (100g) — about 3% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; Putu Piring is one of the lowest-sodium kueh available. Patients with hypertension may consume Putu Piring occasionally without sodium concerns; the very low sodium makes it appropriate as an occasional sweet treat. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Rice flour base has a high GI (~72); gula melaka provides 16.8g sugar per 100g; per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 28 is significant. Diabetic patients should limit Putu Piring to 1–2 pieces maximum as an occasional treat; the high GI rice flour base and palm sugar filling together produce a significant postprandial glucose rise. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High refined carbohydrate and sugar content may promote inflammation at frequent consumption; coconut provides medium-chain saturated fatty acids with neutral-to-positive anti-inflammatory profile. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Putu Piring to occasional consumption; the high sugar content is the primary pro-inflammatory concern. Note: Fully vegan, gluten-free, and allergen-free. Cholesterol and vitamin B12 are 0.0. Nutritionally simple — primarily starch and sugar. The coconut coating adds medium-chain saturated fatty acids (predominantly lauric acid). Must be consumed fresh while warm — does not keep well.

This is **Kueh Tutu (coconut / groundnut filling)**. Kueh Tutu is one of Singapore's most traditional and nostalgic street kueh — small, white dome-shaped steamed cakes made from finely ground rice flour pressed into metal mould plates with a sweet filling of either freshly shredded coconut with sugar, or ground roasted peanuts with sugar. The moulds are placed over a steamer and the kueh is ready in minutes, served fresh in a small banana or pandan leaf wrapper. Kueh Tutu stalls, once common throughout Singapore, are now rare heritage fixtures, making them a sought-after nostalgic treat at cultural events and selected hawker centres. Per 100g: 212.0 kcal, 3.5g protein, 5.2g fat, 39.5g carbohydrates, 2.2g dietary fibre, 78.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 72 (High (≥70)). Glycemic Load per serving: 25.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — fine rice flour, steamed; GI elevated by gelatinised rice starch similar to putu piring. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 70mg per 3-piece serving (90g) — about 3% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; one of the lowest-sodium traditional kueh. Patients with hypertension may consume Kueh Tutu occasionally; the sodium content is negligible. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Fine rice flour base has a high GI (~72); per-serving glycaemic load of approximately 26 from just 3 pieces is significant; sugar from filling adds further glycaemic impact. Diabetic patients should limit Kueh Tutu to 1–2 pieces as an occasional treat; the high GI rice flour base and sweet filling together produce a significant postprandial glucose rise disproportionate to the small portion size. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High refined carbohydrate and sugar content may promote inflammation at frequent consumption; coconut provides medium-chain saturated fatty acids. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Kueh Tutu to occasional treats due to the high sugar and refined rice flour content. Note: Coconut version is listed here. Groundnut version has slightly higher protein (5.5g/100g), higher fat (8.5g/100g), and lower saturated fat ratio. Both versions are fully vegan, gluten-free, and allergen-free (coconut version). This is the final record in Batch 1 of the Project Veritas initiative.

This is **Char Kway Teow**. Char Kway Teow is a beloved Singapore Chinese hawker dish of flat rice noodles (kway teow) stir-fried in a screaming-hot wok with cockles, prawns, Chinese sausage (lap cheong), bean sprouts, garlic chives, and eggs, seasoned generously with dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, and chilli. The dish is traditionally cooked with lard to achieve its characteristic smoky wok hei aroma. It originated among Hokkien and Teochew immigrants in Singapore and Malaysia and remains one of the most popular and culturally significant dishes at hawker centres island-wide. Per 100g: 154.0 kcal, 6.8g protein, 7.2g fat, 17.0g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 528.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 50 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 29.8. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — flat rice noodles (cooked); GI referenced for the rice noodle component; composite dish GI estimated based on noodle proportion. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 1848mg per serving (350g) — about 80% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, contributed by dark soy sauce, Chinese sausage, and cockle seasoning. Patients with hypertension should consume Char Kway Teow only occasionally and request reduced soy sauce, avoiding the lard-based version to control both sodium and saturated fat intake. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Flat rice noodles have a moderate glycaemic index (~50); per-serving carbohydrate load is approximately 60g, moderate for a single meal. Diabetic patients may consume Char Kway Teow in moderation; smaller portions (200g or less) are advisable, and the dish should be requested with extra vegetables and reduced sauce. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High saturated fat from lard and Chinese sausage may promote systemic inflammation; however, cockles and shrimp provide zinc and omega-3 fatty acids. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of Char Kway Teow consumption due to its high saturated fat content from lard and processed meat, and choose versions cooked in vegetable oil where available. Note: The dish is not halal due to pork lard and Chinese sausage. Halal versions exist but use chicken sausage and vegetable oil, with slightly different nutrient profiles. The HPB values represent the traditional preparation. Iron content is notably elevated due to cockles, which are rich in haem iron.

This is **Char Kway Teow**. Char Kway Teow is a beloved Singapore Chinese hawker dish of flat rice noodles (kway teow) stir-fried in a screaming-hot wok with cockles, prawns, Chinese sausage (lap cheong), bean sprouts, garlic chives, and eggs, seasoned generously with dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, and chilli. The dish is traditionally cooked with lard to achieve its characteristic smoky wok hei aroma. It originated among Hokkien and Teochew immigrants in Singapore and Malaysia and remains one of the most popular and culturally significant dishes at hawker centres island-wide. Per 100g: 154.0 kcal, 6.8g protein, 7.2g fat, 17.0g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 528.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 50 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 29.8. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — flat rice noodles (cooked); GI referenced for the rice noodle component; composite dish GI estimated based on noodle proportion. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 1848mg per serving (350g) — about 80% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, contributed by dark soy sauce, Chinese sausage, and cockle seasoning. Patients with hypertension should consume Char Kway Teow only occasionally and request reduced soy sauce, avoiding the lard-based version to control both sodium and saturated fat intake. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Flat rice noodles have a moderate glycaemic index (~50); per-serving carbohydrate load is approximately 60g, moderate for a single meal. Diabetic patients may consume Char Kway Teow in moderation; smaller portions (200g or less) are advisable, and the dish should be requested with extra vegetables and reduced sauce. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High saturated fat from lard and Chinese sausage may promote systemic inflammation; however, cockles and shrimp provide zinc and omega-3 fatty acids. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of Char Kway Teow consumption due to its high saturated fat content from lard and processed meat, and choose versions cooked in vegetable oil where available. Note: The dish is not halal due to pork lard and Chinese sausage. Halal versions exist but use chicken sausage and vegetable oil, with slightly different nutrient profiles. The HPB values represent the traditional preparation. Iron content is notably elevated due to cockles, which are rich in haem iron.

This is **Laksa (Singapore)**. Singapore Laksa is a quintessential Peranakan (Straits Chinese) dish consisting of a rich, spiced coconut milk broth served over rice vermicelli and topped with prawns, halved fish cakes, tofu puffs, cockles, and bean sprouts, finished with fragrant laksa leaf and sambal chilli. The broth is made from a complex paste of lemongrass, galangal, dried shrimp, turmeric, candlenuts, and chilli, simmered with coconut milk to yield a thick, intensely aromatic, mildly spicy soup. It is widely considered one of Singapore's national dishes and is a staple at hawker centres island-wide. Per 100g: 128.0 kcal, 4.6g protein, 7.2g fat, 11.8g carbohydrates, 0.9g dietary fibre, 395.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 47 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 28.8. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — rice vermicelli (cooked); composite dish GI estimated based on noodle and coconut milk proportion. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 2054mg per bowl (520g) — approximately 89% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by shrimp paste, fish sauce, and seasoning in the laksa paste. Hypertensive patients should limit laksa consumption to no more than once per week, request reduced laksa paste, and avoid finishing the broth to significantly reduce sodium intake. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Rice vermicelli has a moderate glycaemic index (~47); however, the high saturated fat from coconut milk slows gastric emptying, moderating the postprandial glucose response. Diabetic patients can consume laksa occasionally with a reduced noodle portion; the high saturated fat content from coconut milk warrants attention for long-term cardiovascular risk management. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High saturated fat content from coconut milk (predominantly lauric acid) and galangal with turmeric in laksa paste, which contain anti-inflammatory compounds. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consume laksa in moderation; while turmeric and galangal in the laksa paste have anti-inflammatory properties, the high saturated fat from coconut milk may contribute to systemic inflammation at high consumption levels. Note: Values represent the standard Singapore coconut laksa preparation. Assam laksa (tamarind-based, Penang style) is nutritionally distinct with significantly lower fat. The HPB entry covers the coconut milk variety. Saturated fat is notably high due to coconut milk.

This is **Nasi Lemak (basic)**. Nasi Lemak is the quintessential breakfast and comfort food of Malaysia and Singapore, consisting of fragrant jasmine rice cooked in coconut milk with pandan leaf, traditionally served wrapped in banana leaf with crispy fried ikan bilis (dried anchovies), roasted peanuts, sliced cucumber, a hard-boiled or fried egg, and spicy sambal chilli paste. The coconut milk imparts a rich, creamy fragrance to the rice and a slightly sweet flavour that balances the heat of the sambal. While originating as a humble Malay breakfast, it is now consumed at all meal times and found at hawker stalls, mamak restaurants, and food courts across Singapore. Per 100g: 176.0 kcal, 4.9g protein, 9.4g fat, 19.2g carbohydrates, 1.0g dietary fibre, 278.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 60 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 43.7. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — white rice cooked in coconut milk has a slightly elevated GI compared to plain boiled rice; estimated based on rice component GI data. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1056mg per serving (380g) — about 46% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, contributed primarily by sambal and ikan bilis. Hypertensive patients should limit the sambal portion and avoid adding extra soy sauce; the basic preparation without added condiments is more sodium-appropriate. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Coconut rice has a high GI (~60) and a high glycaemic load per serving (~44); the coconut milk base increases caloric density without reducing the carbohydrate impact. Diabetic patients should consume Nasi Lemak sparingly, limit rice to half a portion, and prefer the version with more cucumber, egg, and ikan bilis which offer protein and fibre without adding to carbohydrate load. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High saturated fat content from coconut milk may promote pro-inflammatory pathways at high consumption; ikan bilis and peanuts provide calcium and healthy fats respectively. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Nasi Lemak in moderation; the coconut milk saturated fat content warrants limiting frequency, while the omega-3 content of ikan bilis provides some anti-inflammatory benefit. Note: Values represent the basic nasi lemak with ikan bilis, peanuts, cucumber, egg, and sambal only. Versions with fried chicken, rendang, or otah will have significantly higher calorie and fat content. The saturated fat is dominated by coconut milk (medium-chain saturated fatty acids).

The coconut milk imparts a rich, creamy fragrance to the rice and a slightly sweet flavour that balances the heat of the sambal. While originating as a humble Malay breakfast, it is now consumed at all meal times and found at hawker stalls, mamak restaurants, and food courts across Singapore. Per 100g: 176.0 kcal, 4.9g protein, 9.4g fat, 19.2g carbohydrates, 1.0g dietary fibre, 278.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 60 (Medium (56–69)). Source: University of Sydney GI Database — white rice cooked in coconut milk has a slightly elevated GI compared to plain boiled rice; estimated based on rice component GI data. This is **Bak Kut Teh (pork ribs, pepper broth)**. Bak Kut Teh (literally 'meat bone tea') is a Singapore and Malaysian Chinese dish of pork spare ribs slow-simmered in a clear, intensely peppery and garlicky broth for several hours until the meat is fall-off-the-bone tender. The Singapore (Teochew) version is distinguished by its pale, peppery clear broth, contrasting with the darker, herb-rich Klang (Cantonese) variety popular in Malaysia. It is traditionally consumed as a breakfast dish by early-rising labourers but is now equally popular for lunch, dinner, and supper, typically accompanied by steamed white rice, you tiao (dough fritters) for dipping, and a pot of strong oolong or puer tea. Per 100g: 94.0 kcal, 12.2g protein, 4.5g fat, 1.5g carbohydrates, 0.1g dietary fibre, 608.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 3040mg per serving (500g) — significantly exceeding the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by soy sauce seasoning and long-simmered broth. Patients with hypertension should limit Bak Kut Teh consumption and avoid drinking the broth, requesting reduced soy seasoning and relying on the lean pork rib meat only. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Minimal carbohydrate content in the soup itself (~1.5g per 100g); the rice served alongside is the primary glycaemic driver. The Bak Kut Teh soup and pork ribs themselves have minimal carbohydrate impact; diabetic patients should manage portion size of the accompanying steamed rice and avoid you tiao (dough fritters). Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. White pepper and garlic in the broth contain anti-inflammatory phytocompounds; lean pork rib meat provides high-quality protein to support muscle maintenance. Bak Kut Teh is a relatively suitable meal for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its lean pork protein and anti-inflammatory spice profile; sodium from soy seasoning should be managed. Note: Sodium content is highly variable and depends significantly on the amount of soy sauce and salt added during cooking. The Klang (Cantonese) herbal variety will have additional trace minerals from herbs. Confidence upgraded to medium pending exact HPB entry confirmation. Recommend verifying against HPB Foodo if a specific BKT entry is subsequently added.

The Singapore (Teochew) version is distinguished by its pale, peppery clear broth, contrasting with the darker, herb-rich Klang (Cantonese) variety popular in Malaysia. It is traditionally consumed as a breakfast dish by early-rising labourers but is now equally popular for lunch, dinner, and supper, typically accompanied by steamed white rice, you tiao (dough fritters) for dipping, and a pot of strong oolong or puer tea. Per 100g: 94.0 kcal, 12.2g protein, 4.5g fat, 1.5g carbohydrates, 0.1g dietary fibre, 608.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 3040mg per serving (500g) — significantly exceeding the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by soy sauce seasoning and long-simmered broth. Patients with hypertension should limit Bak Kut Teh consumption and avoid drinking the broth, requesting reduced soy seasoning and relying on the lean pork rib meat only. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Minimal carbohydrate content in the soup itself (~1.5g per 100g); the rice served alongside is the primary glycaemic driver. The Bak Kut Teh soup and pork ribs themselves have minimal carbohydrate impact; diabetic patients should manage portion size of the accompanying steamed rice and avoid you tiao (dough fritters). Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. White pepper and garlic in the broth contain anti-inflammatory phytocompounds; lean pork rib meat provides high-quality protein to support muscle maintenance. Bak Kut Teh is a relatively suitable meal for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its lean pork protein and anti-inflammatory spice profile; sodium from soy seasoning should be managed. Note: Sodium content is highly variable and depends significantly on the amount of soy sauce and salt added during cooking. The Klang (Cantonese) herbal variety will have additional trace minerals from herbs. Confidence upgraded to medium pending exact HPB entry confirmation. Recommend verifying against HPB Foodo if a specific BKT entry is subsequently added.

I have taken a photo of my meal. This is **Hokkien Mee**. Singapore Hokkien Mee is a distinctive hawker dish of thick yellow egg noodles and thin rice vermicelli slow-braised in a rich, sweet prawn and pork bone stock until the noodles absorb the flavourful broth, then stir-fried with prawns, squid, pork belly, egg, and bean sprouts. It is served with half a calamansi lime for squeezing, crispy lard bits, and spicy sambal belacan chilli. Unlike the Klang Valley Hokkien Mee (which is dark and soy-sauced), Singapore Hokkien Mee is distinguished by its wet, pale golden broth-braised preparation and is considered one of Singapore's most beloved hawker dishes. Per 100g: 137.0 kcal, 6.3g protein, 5.8g fat, 16.2g carbohydrates, 0.7g dietary fibre, 475.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 52 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 32.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — yellow egg noodles and rice vermicelli mixed; composite GI estimated from noodle proportions. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 1805mg per serving (380g) — about 78% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by prawn stock reduction, soy sauce seasoning, and sambal belacan. Patients with hypertension should limit Hokkien Mee to occasional consumption, request reduced sambal and seasoning, and squeeze calamansi over the dish rather than adding extra soy sauce. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Mixed yellow noodles and bee hoon have a moderate combined GI (~52); per-serving carbohydrate load of approximately 62g is moderate. Diabetic patients can consume Hokkien Mee in moderation with a reduced noodle portion; the seafood components (prawn, squid) add high-quality protein with minimal glycaemic impact. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. Contains squid and prawns which provide omega-3 fatty acids; lard used in traditional preparation adds saturated fat. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Hokkien Mee occasionally; the omega-3 content from seafood is beneficial, but the saturated fat from lard should be managed by requesting oil-fried versions. Note: Not halal due to pork belly and lard. Halal versions substituting chicken and vegetable oil exist at many stalls. Nutrient values may vary significantly depending on the richness of the prawn stock used and the proportion of lard.

This is **Mee Siam**. Mee Siam is a Singapore and Malaysian Malay hawker dish of thin rice vermicelli stir-fried in a tangy, mildly spicy, sweet-sour gravy made from dried shrimp, chilli, tamarind, and coconut milk. The dish is garnished with prawns, halved fried tofu, hard-boiled egg, bean sprouts, and Chinese chives, finished with a squeeze of lime and a drizzle of sambal. It is considered a uniquely Singaporean-Malay interpretation of Thai-influenced noodle cooking, distinct from Thai recipes, and is commonly found at Malay hawker stalls and food courts. Per 100g: 124.0 kcal, 4.9g protein, 4.1g fat, 17.4g carbohydrates, 1.0g dietary fibre, 415.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 47 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 29.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — rice vermicelli (cooked); composite GI estimated. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1494mg per serving (360g) — about 65% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by dried shrimp, belacan paste, and light soy seasoning. Patients with hypertension should request reduced sambal and reduced seasoning; the tamarind-based gravy naturally has some acidity that can balance flavour without additional salt. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Rice vermicelli has a moderate GI (~47); the dish has a moderate glycaemic load per serving (~29); added sugars in the tamarind gravy should be noted. Diabetic patients may consume Mee Siam occasionally with a reduced noodle portion; the tamarind gravy's added sugar content should be accounted for in daily carbohydrate management. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Moderate saturated fat content; tamarind provides antioxidants and the spice base may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefits. Mee Siam is a relatively suitable dish for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the tamarind and spice components may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefits, and the overall fat content is lower than many other hawker dishes. Note: The dish is halal in its standard Malay hawker preparation. Chinese-stall versions may use non-halal ingredients. Sugar content is notably higher than many other noodle dishes due to the sweetened tamarind gravy.

This is **Mee Goreng**. Singapore Mee Goreng is a hawker dish of yellow egg noodles stir-fried in a sweet, spicy, and tangy sauce made from tomato ketchup, chilli paste, and seasonings, cooked with fried tofu cubes, potato cubes, egg, and bean sprouts. It is the signature dish of Indian Muslim (mamak) hawker stalls and is distinctly Singaporean in character — unlike Indonesian mi goreng, the Singapore version is more tomato-based and less sweet. It is garnished with green chillies and a squeeze of lime, and the stir-fry uses high heat to impart a slight char. Per 100g: 142.0 kcal, 5.8g protein, 6.0g fat, 17.0g carbohydrates, 1.2g dietary fibre, 505.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 55 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 33.0. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — yellow wheat noodles (cooked); composite GI estimated. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1768mg per serving (350g) — about 77% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, contributed by soy sauce, tomato ketchup, and sambal seasoning. Patients with hypertension should request reduced sauce and avoid adding extra soy sauce; the tomato ketchup is a significant hidden sodium and sugar source. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Yellow noodles have a moderate GI (~55); fried potato cubes add additional starchy carbohydrates; added sugar from ketchup is notable. Diabetic patients should request reduced ketchup, limit the potato cubes, and keep portions to 200g or less; extra tofu and vegetables can be substituted for the starchy components. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Moderate fat content with a better unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio compared to lard-based noodle dishes; vitamin C from tomatoes and chilli may offer anti-inflammatory support. Mee Goreng is a relatively suitable hawker choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its lower saturated fat content and vitamin C from the tomato-based sauce. Note: Halal at Indian Muslim (mamak) stalls. Chinese-style fried mee will differ in ingredients and nutritional profile. The tomato ketchup and sambal contribute both hidden sugar and sodium. Shrimp or chicken versions will increase protein and seafood allergen content.

This is **Wonton Mee (dry)**. Wonton Mee (dry) is a classic Singapore Chinese hawker dish of thin, springy egg noodles tossed in a sauce of oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, chilli, and lard, topped with sliced char siu barbecue pork, three to four plump pork-and-shrimp wontons, and blanched choy sum. A small bowl of clear, delicate wonton broth is served alongside. The noodles are distinguished by their slight alkaline bounce from the kansui (lye water) used in their production. It is one of the most enduring Cantonese-influenced dishes at Singapore hawker centres. Per 100g: 154.0 kcal, 7.2g protein, 5.9g fat, 20.2g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 558.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 46 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 31.7. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — thin wheat egg noodles (cooked); composite GI estimated. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 1897mg per serving (340g) — about 82% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, and seasoned char siu. Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce tossing and ask for char siu portion to be limited; avoiding the additional chilli sauce tossing will also reduce sodium significantly. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Thin egg noodles have a low-moderate GI (~46); per-serving carbohydrate load of approximately 69g is moderate-high for a single meal. Diabetic patients should consider a half-portion of noodles with extra greens and wontons; the low GI of the thin egg noodles is favourable compared to rice-based alternatives. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Moderate saturated fat from lard; choy sum provides folate and anti-inflammatory antioxidants; overall dish is lower in inflammation markers than deep-fried hawker alternatives. Wonton Mee is a reasonable meal choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; requesting the version with reduced lard and extra blanched vegetables improves the anti-inflammatory nutritional profile. Note: Not halal due to pork (char siu, wonton filling) and lard. Halal chicken char siu versions exist. Sodium values are highly variable depending on the amount of soy sauce and oyster sauce used in tossing. A wet (soup) version will have higher sodium from the broth.

This is **Wonton Mee (dry)**. Wonton Mee (dry) is a classic Singapore Chinese hawker dish of thin, springy egg noodles tossed in a sauce of oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, chilli, and lard, topped with sliced char siu barbecue pork, three to four plump pork-and-shrimp wontons, and blanched choy sum. A small bowl of clear, delicate wonton broth is served alongside. The noodles are distinguished by their slight alkaline bounce from the kansui (lye water) used in their production. It is one of the most enduring Cantonese-influenced dishes at Singapore hawker centres. Per 100g: 154.0 kcal, 7.2g protein, 5.9g fat, 20.2g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 558.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 46 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 31.7. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — thin wheat egg noodles (cooked); composite GI estimated. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 1897mg per serving (340g) — about 82% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, and seasoned char siu. Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce tossing and ask for char siu portion to be limited; avoiding the additional chilli sauce tossing will also reduce sodium significantly. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Thin egg noodles have a low-moderate GI (~46); per-serving carbohydrate load of approximately 69g is moderate-high for a single meal. Diabetic patients should consider a half-portion of noodles with extra greens and wontons; the low GI of the thin egg noodles is favourable compared to rice-based alternatives. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Moderate saturated fat from lard; choy sum provides folate and anti-inflammatory antioxidants; overall dish is lower in inflammation markers than deep-fried hawker alternatives. Wonton Mee is a reasonable meal choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; requesting the version with reduced lard and extra blanched vegetables improves the anti-inflammatory nutritional profile. Note: Not halal due to pork (char siu, wonton filling) and lard. Halal chicken char siu versions exist. Sodium values are highly variable depending on the amount of soy sauce and oyster sauce used in tossing. A wet (soup) version will have higher sodium from the broth.

This is **Yong Tau Foo (steamed, clear soup)**. Yong Tau Foo is a Singapore Chinese hawker dish of Hakka origin consisting of tofu, vegetables (bitter gourd, brinjal, chilli, ladies fingers), fish cakes, and other items stuffed with or coated in a savoury fish paste, then steamed, boiled, or fried. At Singapore hawker stalls, diners self-select 8–10 items from a display, which are served in a clear, light anchovy or pork broth over noodles or rice. The dish is known for being one of the most balanced and nutritious hawker options, with high protein, abundant vegetables, and relatively low fat content. Per 100g: 74.0 kcal, 7.4g protein, 2.7g fat, 6.2g carbohydrates, 1.5g dietary fibre, 348.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 40 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 12.4. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — rice noodles / vermicelli; low GI estimated due to high proportion of vegetables, tofu, and fish paste relative to noodles. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1740mg per bowl (500g) — about 76% of daily recommended limit, driven primarily by the broth and the dipping sauces served alongside. Patients with hypertension should avoid drinking the broth and request reduced or no sweet sauce and chilli, significantly lowering sodium intake while retaining the high protein and vegetable benefits. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Low GI (~40) and low glycaemic load per serving (~12); high protein from fish paste and tofu moderates the postprandial glucose response; vegetables add fibre. Yong Tau Foo is one of the most diabetes-friendly hawker options available; patients should select items heavy in tofu and vegetables, limit the noodle base, and avoid the sweet thick sauce topping. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Low fat content, high protein from fish paste (containing omega-3 fatty acids), and abundant vegetables make this one of the most anti-inflammatory hawker options. Yong Tau Foo in clear soup is an excellent meal choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the fish paste provides omega-3 fatty acids and the diverse vegetable selection delivers antioxidants and anti-inflammatory phytonutrients. Note: Nutritional content is highly variable depending on individual item selection. Deep-fried YTF items (e.g., fried fish cake, fried wonton) will significantly increase fat and calorie content. The sweet black sauce topping adds approximately 60–80 kcal of sugar. The clear soup version is significantly lower in calories than the laksa-based version.

This is **Fried Carrot Cake / Chai Tow Kway (black)**. Chai Tow Kway (Singapore Fried Carrot Cake) is a popular hawker dish made from white radish (daikon) steamed with rice flour to form a soft cake, which is then cubed and stir-fried in a hot wok with egg, preserved radish (chai poh), garlic, and spring onions. The black version gets its distinctive sweet and savoury caramelised coating from the liberal addition of dark sweet soy sauce, giving the cubes a glossy, deeply flavoured char. Despite being called 'carrot cake', it contains no orange carrot — the name derives from the Teochew word 'chai tow' meaning white radish, which was mistranslated as 'carrot' in English. Per 100g: 163.0 kcal, 3.9g protein, 8.4g fat, 19.1g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 618.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 62 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 35.4. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — rice flour-based product; GI estimated from rice cake and rice flour dish data. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 1854mg per serving (300g) — about 81% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by preserved radish (chai poh), dark soy sauce, and seasonings. Patients with hypertension should consume Chai Tow Kway only occasionally and request reduced dark soy sauce and preserved radish to meaningfully lower sodium intake. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Rice flour cake base has a moderately high GI (~62); the black version adds significant sugar from dark soy sauce caramelisation, increasing glycaemic load to approximately 35 per serving. Diabetic patients should prefer the white version (no dark soy sauce) over the black version, as the caramelised sugar coating in the black version substantially increases the glycaemic impact. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High sodium from preserved vegetables and soy sauce; lard use adds saturated fat; limited anti-inflammatory micronutrient content. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption of Chai Tow Kway due to its high sodium and saturated fat content, and prefer dishes with greater vegetable diversity. Note: Not halal at traditional Chinese stalls due to lard use. Halal versions using vegetable oil are available. White version (no dark soy sauce) has significantly less sugar and slightly lower sodium. The dish is often misidentified by tourists as containing orange carrot due to the English name.

This is **Prawn Noodle Soup (Hokkien Prawn Mee)**. Singapore Prawn Noodle Soup (Hae Mee) is a hawker classic of yellow egg noodles and rice vermicelli served in a deeply flavourful prawn and pork rib stock that has been simmered for hours with prawn shells, lemongrass, and spices to develop an intensely sweet, umami-rich broth. The bowl is topped with fresh whole prawns, fork-tender pork ribs, thin pork belly slices, half a hard-boiled egg, blanched bean sprouts and kangkung, and crispy fried shallots, with a side of house-made sambal belacan. It is one of Singapore's most enduring hawker dishes, associated with early morning queues and long-established family stalls. Per 100g: 117.0 kcal, 7.3g protein, 3.9g fat, 14.5g carbohydrates, 1.0g dietary fibre, 575.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 51 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 41.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — mixed noodles (egg noodles + rice vermicelli); composite GI estimated. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 3220mg per bowl (560g) — significantly exceeding the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by the long-simmered prawn and pork broth. Patients with hypertension should avoid finishing the broth and request reduced sambal belacan; consuming only the noodles and toppings while leaving most of the broth will substantially reduce sodium intake. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Mixed noodle base has a moderate GI (~51); the large bowl serving size means a moderate-high carbohydrate load of approximately 81g. Diabetic patients should request a half-portion of noodles and additional kangkung, and consider the pork ribs and prawns as the primary nutritional focus rather than the noodle base. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Prawns provide omega-3 fatty acids; kangkung adds anti-inflammatory antioxidants; pork rib protein supports muscle maintenance. Prawn Noodle Soup is a suitable meal for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the prawn and pork protein content is beneficial, and patients should focus on the solid ingredients while limiting the high-sodium broth. Note: Not halal due to pork ribs and pork belly. Halal prawn mee with chicken substitutes exists. Sodium from broth is very high — the broth alone accounts for the majority of sodium. A dry (gon lo) version with less broth would be lower in sodium.

This is **Prawn Noodle Soup (Hokkien Prawn Mee)**. Singapore Prawn Noodle Soup (Hae Mee) is a hawker classic of yellow egg noodles and rice vermicelli served in a deeply flavourful prawn and pork rib stock that has been simmered for hours with prawn shells, lemongrass, and spices to develop an intensely sweet, umami-rich broth. The bowl is topped with fresh whole prawns, fork-tender pork ribs, thin pork belly slices, half a hard-boiled egg, blanched bean sprouts and kangkung, and crispy fried shallots, with a side of house-made sambal belacan. It is one of Singapore's most enduring hawker dishes, associated with early morning queues and long-established family stalls. Per 100g: 117.0 kcal, 7.3g protein, 3.9g fat, 14.5g carbohydrates, 1.0g dietary fibre, 575.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 51 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 41.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — mixed noodles (egg noodles + rice vermicelli); composite GI estimated. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 3220mg per bowl (560g) — significantly exceeding the 2300mg daily recommended limit, driven by the long-simmered prawn and pork broth. Patients with hypertension should avoid finishing the broth and request reduced sambal belacan; consuming only the noodles and toppings while leaving most of the broth will substantially reduce sodium intake. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Mixed noodle base has a moderate GI (~51); the large bowl serving size means a moderate-high carbohydrate load of approximately 81g. Diabetic patients should request a half-portion of noodles and additional kangkung, and consider the pork ribs and prawns as the primary nutritional focus rather than the noodle base. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Prawns provide omega-3 fatty acids; kangkung adds anti-inflammatory antioxidants; pork rib protein supports muscle maintenance. Prawn Noodle Soup is a suitable meal for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the prawn and pork protein content is beneficial, and patients should focus on the solid ingredients while limiting the high-sodium broth. Note: Not halal due to pork ribs and pork belly. Halal prawn mee with chicken substitutes exists. Sodium from broth is very high — the broth alone accounts for the majority of sodium. A dry (gon lo) version with less broth would be lower in sodium.

This is **Ban Mian (dry, with minced pork and egg)**. Ban Mian is a Singaporean Chinese hawker dish of thick, chewy handmade flat wheat noodles (stretched or torn by hand and boiled to order) served dry-tossed with a sauce of lard, oyster sauce, and dark soy sauce, topped with seasoned minced pork, a soft poached egg, crispy fried ikan bilis (dried anchovies), fried shallots, and blanched leafy greens such as mani cai or spinach. A small bowl of clear, flavourful anchovy-based broth accompanies the dish. The handmade noodles give ban mian its distinctive chewy texture and irregular shape that absorbs sauce well. Per 100g: 147.0 kcal, 8.1g protein, 5.9g fat, 17.5g carbohydrates, 1.2g dietary fibre, 492.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 48 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 31.9. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — handmade wheat noodles (boiled); GI lower than dried/extruded noodles due to denser starch structure. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1870mg per bowl (380g) — about 81% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, contributed by oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, and fried ikan bilis. Patients with hypertension should request reduced dark soy and oyster sauce tossing; the ikan bilis topping, while flavourful, is a significant sodium contributor and can be reduced. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Handmade flat noodles have a lower GI (~48) than standard extruded noodles; minced pork and egg provide protein that moderates postprandial glucose response; leafy greens add fibre. Ban Mian (dry) is a relatively diabetes-friendly hawker noodle option; the handmade noodle's lower GI and the high protein content from pork and egg help moderate blood glucose response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Leafy greens (mani cai or spinach) provide folate and anti-inflammatory antioxidants; moderate protein from minced pork supports muscle maintenance. Ban Mian is a suitable dish for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the leafy greens provide anti-inflammatory micronutrients, and requesting reduced lard will lower the saturated fat content. Note: Not halal at traditional Chinese stalls due to pork and lard. Halal chicken ban mian exists. The handmade noodles' GI advantage over commercial extruded noodles is clinically relevant for diabetic patients. Mee hoon kueh (irregular torn pieces) is a variant of ban mian with identical ingredients.

This is **Bak Chor Mee (minced pork noodle, dry)**. Bak Chor Mee is a beloved Singapore Chinese hawker dish of thin egg noodles or flat noodles tossed with a uniquely tangy-savoury sauce of vinegar, chilli, lard, and soy, topped with seasoned minced pork, silky sliced pork liver, pork balls, braised shiitake mushrooms, and blanched greens. The distinctive tang from black vinegar sets this dish apart from other Singapore noodle preparations. The pork liver in the dish makes it exceptionally rich in vitamin B12, folate, and iron. A small bowl of clear pork broth is served alongside for sipping. Per 100g: 153.0 kcal, 7.9g protein, 6.6g fat, 17.8g carbohydrates, 0.9g dietary fibre, 568.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 46 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 29.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — thin egg noodles (boiled); GI estimated. Hypertension — Concern level: High. Sodium approximately 2045mg per serving (360g) — about 89% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, contributed by dark soy sauce, lard-based seasoning, and pork balls. Patients with hypertension should request reduced dark soy and chilli sauce tossing; pork balls and pork liver are notable sodium contributors and can be reduced in quantity. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Thin egg noodles have a low-moderate GI (~46); per-serving carbohydrate load approximately 64g is moderate; the vinegar in the tossing may modestly blunt the postprandial glucose response. Diabetic patients can consume Bak Chor Mee in moderation; the acetic acid (vinegar) in the characteristic tossing sauce has evidence for modestly reducing the glycaemic response of the noodles. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Pork liver is exceptional for vitamin B12, folate, iron, and vitamin A; the overall dish has moderate protein for muscle support; shiitake mushrooms provide ergothioneine and beta-glucans. Bak Chor Mee provides excellent micronutrient density from pork liver, making it beneficial for patients with rheumatoid arthritis managing anaemia or nutritional deficiencies; saturated fat from lard should be monitored. Note: Not halal due to pork and lard. Vitamin A and vitamin B12 values are notably elevated due to pork liver inclusion; these are clinically significant values. Cholesterol from pork liver is high (~320mg/100g for liver). The vinegar characteristic of BCM has documented glycaemic-modulating effects.

This is **Bak Chor Mee (minced pork noodle, dry)**. Bak Chor Mee is a beloved Singapore Chinese hawker dish of thin egg noodles or flat noodles tossed with a uniquely tangy-savoury sauce of vinegar, chilli, lard, and soy, topped with seasoned minced pork, silky sliced pork liver, pork balls, braised shiitake mushrooms, and blanched greens. The distinctive tang from black vinegar sets this dish apart from other Singapore noodle preparations. The pork liver in the dish makes it exceptionally rich in vitamin B12, folate, and iron. This is **Chicken Satay (with ketupat and peanut sauce)**. Singapore Chicken Satay consists of small cubes of marinated chicken thigh threaded onto bamboo skewers and charcoal-grilled to produce lightly charred, juicy, fragrant morsels seasoned with lemongrass, turmeric, galangal, cumin, and coriander. The skewers are served with a rich, sweet-spicy peanut dipping sauce (satay sauce), compressed rice cakes (ketupat), cucumber slices, and raw red onion wedges. Satay is a pan-Southeast-Asian dish with roots in Malay, Javanese, and Sumatran cuisine, and is one of the most recognisable Singapore hawker dishes, associated with Lau Pa Sat and Gluttons' Bay satay street stalls. Per 100g: 218.0 kcal, 13.8g protein, 11.5g fat, 15.8g carbohydrates, 1.5g dietary fibre, 418.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 50 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 31.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — ketupat (compressed rice cake) GI estimated from white rice data; peanut sauce has a low GI; composite estimated. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1672mg per serving (400g) — about 73% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, contributed primarily by the peanut sauce seasoning and marinade. Patients with hypertension should limit the quantity of peanut dipping sauce and avoid additional soy seasoning; grilled chicken skewers alone are a relatively lean, lower-sodium protein source. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Ketupat (compressed rice) has a moderate-high GI; per-serving carbohydrate load of approximately 63g is moderate; peanut sauce adds healthy fats that slow glucose absorption. Diabetic patients can enjoy chicken satay focusing on the grilled chicken skewers and limiting ketupat to one cube; peanuts in the sauce provide protein and healthy fats that moderate glycaemic impact. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Turmeric and lemongrass in the satay marinade provide anti-inflammatory curcumin and phytochemicals; peanuts add healthy unsaturated fats; grilled chicken is a lean protein source. Chicken satay is a beneficial meal choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the turmeric-rich marinade provides curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties, and the grilled preparation is lower in inflammatory fatty acids than fried alternatives. Note: Peanut sauce is a major allergen concern for nut-allergic patients. The serving is inclusive of ketupat and peanut sauce. Pure chicken skewers alone (no ketupat or sauce) would be significantly lower in carbohydrates (~3g per 100g) and calories (~185 kcal/100g).

This is **Beef Rendang (with steamed rice)**. Beef Rendang is a Malaysian-Indonesian Malay slow-braised dish of beef simmered in a thick, spiced coconut milk paste of lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaf, turmeric, chilli, and toasted grated coconut (kerisik) until nearly all the liquid has evaporated and the beef is coated in a dark, caramelised, intensely aromatic dry crust. The dish is served over steamed rice and is one of the richest, most complex flavoured dishes in SEA cuisine. Originating from the Minangkabau people of West Sumatra, it has been adopted as a signature Malay dish in Singapore and Malaysia. Per 100g: 192.0 kcal, 11.5g protein, 10.5g fat, 16.2g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 382.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 55 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 35.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — white jasmine rice (main GI contributor in the combined dish); beef rendang itself has negligible GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1528mg per serving (400g) — about 66% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, contributed by seasoning salt and soy in the rendang paste. Patients with hypertension should limit the rendang portion to one serving and avoid additional salt or soy sauce on the rice; the coconut-based gravy residue should also be moderated. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Rice component drives the glycaemic load (~36 per serving); rendang itself is low in carbohydrates but high in saturated fat from coconut milk. Diabetic patients should reduce the rice portion to 150g and increase the rendang beef component; the high fat content of rendang will slow gastric emptying and moderate glucose absorption from the rice. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High saturated fat content from coconut milk and kerisik; turmeric and galangal provide anti-inflammatory curcumin and phytocompounds. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Beef Rendang in moderation; while the spice paste provides anti-inflammatory curcumin, the high saturated fat from coconut milk warrants limiting consumption frequency. Note: The rendang component alone (without rice) has approximately 280 kcal/100g and very low carbohydrate content (~3g/100g). The combined serving with rice drives the carbohydrate and glycaemic impact. Saturated fat from coconut milk is predominantly medium-chain (lauric acid), which has different metabolic effects than long-chain saturated fatty acids.

Originating from the Minangkabau people of West Sumatra, it has been adopted as a signature Malay dish in Singapore and Malaysia. Per 100g: 192.0 kcal, 11.5g protein, 10.5g fat, 16.2g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 382.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 55 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 35.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — white jasmine rice (main GI contributor in the combined dish); beef rendang itself has negligible GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1528mg per serving (400g) — about 66% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit, contributed by seasoning salt and soy in the rendang paste. Patients with hypertension should limit the rendang portion to one serving and avoid additional salt or soy sauce on the rice; the coconut-based gravy residue should also be moderated. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Rice component drives the glycaemic load (~36 per serving); rendang itself is low in carbohydrates but high in saturated fat from coconut milk. Diabetic patients should reduce the rice portion to 150g and increase the rendang beef component; the high fat content of rendang will slow gastric emptying and moderate glucose absorption from the rice. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High saturated fat content from coconut milk and kerisik; turmeric and galangal provide anti-inflammatory curcumin and phytocompounds. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Beef Rendang in moderation; while the spice paste provides anti-inflammatory curcumin, the high saturated fat from coconut milk warrants limiting consumption frequency. Note: The rendang component alone (without rice) has approximately 280 kcal/100g and very low carbohydrate content (~3g/100g). The combined serving with rice drives the carbohydrate and glycaemic impact. Saturated fat from coconut milk is predominantly medium-chain (lauric acid), which has different metabolic effects than long-chain saturated fatty acids.

This is **Otah Otah (grilled fish cake in banana leaf)**. Otah Otah (commonly called 'otah' in Singapore) is a Southeast Asian snack of fresh mackerel blended into a smooth spiced paste with coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, chilli, and belacan, mixed with egg to bind, wrapped tightly in folded banana leaf, and grilled over charcoal until the fish paste sets and the banana leaf chars slightly, imparting a smoky, herbal fragrance. It is served as an accompaniment to rice or as a standalone snack, and is particularly popular in Singapore and Johor (the Muar-style steamed version is also widely available). Otah is distinguished by its high omega-3 content from mackerel and its anti-inflammatory spice profile. Per 100g: 133.0 kcal, 13.2g protein, 7.5g fat, 3.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 508.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 406mg per piece (80g) — one piece represents about 18% of the daily limit; multiple pieces (3–4 commonly consumed) would bring intake to 70–75% of the daily limit. Patients with hypertension may consume 1–2 pieces of otah otah as a protein accompaniment; the shrimp paste (belacan) and seasoning salt are the primary sodium sources and increase significantly with portion size. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low carbohydrate content (~3.5g/100g); high protein from mackerel with minimal glycaemic impact; one of the lowest-GI hawker snacks available. Otah Otah is an excellent protein snack for diabetic patients due to its very low carbohydrate content, high-quality fish protein, and omega-3 fatty acids that support insulin sensitivity. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Spanish mackerel is rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) with anti-inflammatory effects; turmeric and galangal in the spice paste provide curcumin and anti-inflammatory phytocompounds. Otah Otah is an excellent food for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the omega-3 rich mackerel base and anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, galangal, lemongrass) make it one of the most beneficial traditional SEA hawker foods for inflammatory conditions. Note: Vitamin D and vitamin B12 values are notably high, reflecting the mackerel base. Selenium content is also elevated from fish. The banana leaf wrapper is not consumed. Muar-style (steamed, not grilled) may have slightly different texture and lower char compounds.

Otah Otah (commonly called 'otah' in Singapore) is a Southeast Asian snack of fresh mackerel blended into a smooth spiced paste with coconut milk, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, chilli, and belacan, mixed with egg to bind, wrapped tightly in folded banana leaf, and grilled over charcoal until the fish paste sets and the banana leaf chars slightly, imparting a smoky, herbal fragrance. It is served as an accompaniment to rice or as a standalone snack, and is particularly popular in Singapore and Johor (the Muar-style steamed version is also widely available). Otah is distinguished by its high omega-3 content from mackerel and its anti-inflammatory spice profile. Per 100g: 133.0 kcal, 13.2g protein, 7.5g fat, 3.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 508.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 406mg per piece (80g) — one piece represents about 18% of the daily limit; multiple pieces (3–4 commonly consumed) would bring intake to 70–75% of the daily limit. Patients with hypertension may consume 1–2 pieces of otah otah as a protein accompaniment; the shrimp paste (belacan) and seasoning salt are the primary sodium sources and increase significantly with portion size. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low carbohydrate content (~3.5g/100g); high protein from mackerel with minimal glycaemic impact; one of the lowest-GI hawker snacks available. Otah Otah is an excellent protein snack for diabetic patients due to its very low carbohydrate content, high-quality fish protein, and omega-3 fatty acids that support insulin sensitivity. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Spanish mackerel is rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) with anti-inflammatory effects; turmeric and galangal in the spice paste provide curcumin and anti-inflammatory phytocompounds. Otah Otah is an excellent food for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the omega-3 rich mackerel base and anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, galangal, lemongrass) make it one of the most beneficial traditional SEA hawker foods for inflammatory conditions. Note: Vitamin D and vitamin B12 values are notably high, reflecting the mackerel base. Selenium content is also elevated from fish. The banana leaf wrapper is not consumed. Muar-style (steamed, not grilled) may have slightly different texture and lower char compounds.

This is **Popiah (fresh, Singaporean style)**. Singapore Popiah is a fresh (not fried) spring roll of Teochew-Hokkien and Peranakan origin, consisting of a thin, soft wheat flour skin wrapped around a generous filling of slowly braised jicama (bangkuang) and carrot, bean sprouts, fried tofu strips, egg omelette strips, small prawns, and crushed roasted peanuts, smeared with hoisin sauce, sweet dark sauce, and chilli paste. Unlike the deep-fried spring roll, the fresh popiah is a lighter, more vegetable-forward dish, and is regarded as one of Singapore's most distinctive and nutritionally balanced hawker snacks. Per 100g: 119.0 kcal, 5.2g protein, 4.0g fat, 16.0g carbohydrates, 2.2g dietary fibre, 308.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 48 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 21.5. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — thin wheat flour wrap; GI modified downward by high vegetable content of filling. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 862mg per 2-roll serving (280g) — about 37% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; one of the lower-sodium hawker options. Patients with hypertension may consume popiah regularly; the main sodium contributors are the hoisin and sweet black sauce — requesting reduced sauce will further lower sodium intake. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Low-moderate GI (~48); high vegetable fibre from jicama and bean sprouts moderates glucose absorption; glycaemic load per serving is low (~22). Fresh Popiah is one of the most diabetes-friendly hawker snacks; the jicama filling is naturally low in carbohydrates and high in fibre, and the sweet sauces are the primary glycaemic driver that can be reduced. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. High vegetable content from jicama, bean sprouts, and lettuce provides anti-inflammatory antioxidants; low saturated fat makes this one of the most anti-inflammatory hawker choices. Fresh Popiah is an excellent food choice for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the high vegetable content, low saturated fat, and anti-inflammatory vitamin C content make it one of the most suitable hawker options. Note: Not halal at Chinese stalls due to Chinese sausage (lap cheong) and possible pork in the filling. Halal versions without pork-derived ingredients are available. The fresh version has significantly fewer calories than the deep-fried version. Peanuts are a critical allergen topping.

This is **Curry Puff (baked, potato filling)**. The Singapore Curry Puff is one of the most beloved local snacks, consisting of a flaky, multi-layered baked wheat flour pastry shell (made with butter or margarine for its characteristic spiral layers) filled with a mildly spiced potato curry seasoned with turmeric, curry powder, onions, and sometimes diced chicken or hard-boiled egg. The baked version is distinguished from the fried epok-epok by its golden, oven-baked shell. It is a staple of traditional bakeries, kiosks (Old Chang Kee is the iconic chain), and hawker stall breakfast menus across Singapore and Malaysia. Per 100g: 285.0 kcal, 5.6g protein, 15.0g fat, 33.0g carbohydrates, 2.0g dietary fibre, 378.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 65 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 19.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — wheat flour pastry base; GI elevated by refined flour shell; potato filling adds moderate GI starch. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 340mg per piece (90g) — about 15% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; relatively moderate sodium for a savory snack. Patients with hypertension may consume a single curry puff occasionally; the sodium content is moderate, but the high saturated fat and calorie density make it a poor choice for frequent snacking. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Refined wheat pastry has a high GI (~65); per-piece carbohydrate load is approximately 30g; high fat content from butter/margarine slows but does not prevent significant postprandial glucose rise. Diabetic patients should limit curry puff consumption to occasional treats; the refined flour pastry shell and potato filling together produce a moderate-high glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High saturated fat from butter or margarine in the pastry; refined flour has a pro-inflammatory glycaemic impact; curry spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric) provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit curry puff consumption due to the high refined flour and saturated fat content; the anti-inflammatory spices in the filling provide minimal offsetting benefit. Note: Baked version has ~20% fewer calories than the deep-fried version. Versions with egg or chicken will have higher protein. Vegetarian in standard potato filling without meat; contains egg and dairy (butter) so not vegan. Trans fat content depends on whether margarine with partially hydrogenated fat is used.

The Singapore Curry Puff is one of the most beloved local snacks, consisting of a flaky, multi-layered baked wheat flour pastry shell (made with butter or margarine for its characteristic spiral layers) filled with a mildly spiced potato curry seasoned with turmeric, curry powder, onions, and sometimes diced chicken or hard-boiled egg. The baked version is distinguished from the fried epok-epok by its golden, oven-baked shell. It is a staple of traditional bakeries, kiosks (Old Chang Kee is the iconic chain), and hawker stall breakfast menus across Singapore and Malaysia. Per 100g: 285.0 kcal, 5.6g protein, 15.0g fat, 33.0g carbohydrates, 2.0g dietary fibre, 378.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 65 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 19.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — wheat flour pastry base; GI elevated by refined flour shell; potato filling adds moderate GI starch. Hypertension — Concern level: Low. Sodium approximately 340mg per piece (90g) — about 15% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; relatively moderate sodium for a savory snack. Patients with hypertension may consume a single curry puff occasionally; the sodium content is moderate, but the high saturated fat and calorie density make it a poor choice for frequent snacking. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Refined wheat pastry has a high GI (~65); per-piece carbohydrate load is approximately 30g; high fat content from butter/margarine slows but does not prevent significant postprandial glucose rise. Diabetic patients should limit curry puff consumption to occasional treats; the refined flour pastry shell and potato filling together produce a moderate-high glycaemic response. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. High saturated fat from butter or margarine in the pastry; refined flour has a pro-inflammatory glycaemic impact; curry spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric) provide modest anti-inflammatory benefit. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit curry puff consumption due to the high refined flour and saturated fat content; the anti-inflammatory spices in the filling provide minimal offsetting benefit. Note: Baked version has ~20% fewer calories than the deep-fried version. Versions with egg or chicken will have higher protein. Vegetarian in standard potato filling without meat; contains egg and dairy (butter) so not vegan. Trans fat content depends on whether margarine with partially hydrogenated fat is used.

This is **Mutton Biryani**. Mutton Biryani is a rich, aromatic Indian rice dish of slow-braised bone-in mutton layered with saffron-infused basmati rice and cooked dum style (sealed with dough or foil) to allow the steam to perfume the rice with the spiced mutton juices. The mutton is marinated in yoghurt, biryani masala, ginger-garlic paste, and caramelised onions before cooking, resulting in fall-off-the-bone tender meat with a complex, deeply spiced flavour. It is one of the most popular dishes at Singapore's Indian Muslim restaurants and is associated with celebratory meals. Per 100g: 180.0 kcal, 10.5g protein, 8.2g fat, 17.8g carbohydrates, 0.8g dietary fibre, 448.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 55 (Low (≤55)). Glycemic Load per serving: 44.1. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — basmati rice (cooked); same GI basis as chicken biryani. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 2016mg per serving (450g) — about 88% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; slightly higher than chicken biryani due to longer mutton braising with more salt. Patients with hypertension should limit accompaniment gravies and request reduced salt in the biryani preparation; mutton biryani is a higher-sodium meal that should be consumed occasionally. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Basmati rice GI of ~55 is more favourable than jasmine rice; per-serving carbohydrate load approximately 80g is substantial; mutton fat slows gastric emptying. Diabetic patients can consume Mutton Biryani in moderation; basmati rice is preferred over jasmine rice for its lower GI, and requesting extra mutton with reduced rice improves the glycaemic outcome. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. Mutton contains arachidonic acid which can promote pro-inflammatory pathways at high consumption; however, the biryani spice profile provides anti-inflammatory compounds. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat frequency; Mutton Biryani consumed 1–2 times weekly is acceptable, but the anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon) provide benefit even with moderate consumption. Note: Iron and vitamin B12 content are notably higher in mutton biryani compared to chicken biryani, reflecting the red meat component. Zinc content is also elevated. In Singapore Indian Muslim restaurants, 'mutton' typically refers to goat meat rather than sheep, which may have slightly different fat profiles.

This is **Butter Chicken (murgh makhani, with rice)**. Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani) is a popular North Indian dish of tandoor-cooked chicken pieces simmered in a mildly spiced, velvety sauce made from puréed tomatoes, cream, butter, cashew paste, ginger, garlic, and aromatic spices including garam masala and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek). The sauce has a characteristically rich, orange-red colour and a mild, creamy sweetness balanced by warm spices. It was reportedly invented at a Delhi restaurant in the 1950s and is now one of the most widely consumed Indian dishes globally, available at every Indian restaurant in Singapore. Per 100g: 143.0 kcal, 8.8g protein, 5.8g fat, 15.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 378.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 58 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 40.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — white jasmine rice (main GI contributor); butter chicken sauce has negligible GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1701mg per serving (450g) — about 74% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; the creamy sauce contains salt, butter, and cream as significant contributors. Patients with hypertension should request reduced salt in the butter chicken preparation; the cream and butter content is cardiovascularly significant but the sodium level is the primary hypertension concern. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Jasmine rice base has a moderate-high GI (~58); the cream and fat content in the sauce slows gastric emptying, moderately blunting the glycaemic response. Diabetic patients should reduce the rice portion and focus on the chicken; the fat content of the sauce provides some glycaemic modulation, making this a moderate choice compared to plain rice dishes. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. Butter and cream are sources of saturated fat and arachidonic acid; however, turmeric and garam masala in the sauce provide anti-inflammatory curcumin. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consume Butter Chicken in moderation; the cream and butter add saturated fat that can promote inflammation, but the anti-inflammatory spice profile partially offsets this. Note: Contains dairy (cream, butter) — not suitable for lactose-intolerant or dairy-free patients. The restaurant versions vary significantly in cream and butter quantity; home-cooked versions may have less fat. Cashew nut allergen is a significant concern.

Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani) is a popular North Indian dish of tandoor-cooked chicken pieces simmered in a mildly spiced, velvety sauce made from puréed tomatoes, cream, butter, cashew paste, ginger, garlic, and aromatic spices including garam masala and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek). The sauce has a characteristically rich, orange-red colour and a mild, creamy sweetness balanced by warm spices. It was reportedly invented at a Delhi restaurant in the 1950s and is now one of the most widely consumed Indian dishes globally, available at every Indian restaurant in Singapore. Per 100g: 143.0 kcal, 8.8g protein, 5.8g fat, 15.5g carbohydrates, 0.5g dietary fibre, 378.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 58 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 40.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — white jasmine rice (main GI contributor); butter chicken sauce has negligible GI. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1701mg per serving (450g) — about 74% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; the creamy sauce contains salt, butter, and cream as significant contributors. Patients with hypertension should request reduced salt in the butter chicken preparation; the cream and butter content is cardiovascularly significant but the sodium level is the primary hypertension concern. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Moderate. Jasmine rice base has a moderate-high GI (~58); the cream and fat content in the sauce slows gastric emptying, moderately blunting the glycaemic response. Diabetic patients should reduce the rice portion and focus on the chicken; the fat content of the sauce provides some glycaemic modulation, making this a moderate choice compared to plain rice dishes. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. Butter and cream are sources of saturated fat and arachidonic acid; however, turmeric and garam masala in the sauce provide anti-inflammatory curcumin. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consume Butter Chicken in moderation; the cream and butter add saturated fat that can promote inflammation, but the anti-inflammatory spice profile partially offsets this. Note: Contains dairy (cream, butter) — not suitable for lactose-intolerant or dairy-free patients. The restaurant versions vary significantly in cream and butter quantity; home-cooked versions may have less fat. Cashew nut allergen is a significant concern.

This is **Roti Prata (plain, with dhal curry)**. Roti Prata is Singapore's beloved Indian Muslim flatbread — a thin, supple dough of white flour, water, salt, and ghee that is stretched, folded multiple times to create layers, then tossed into the air to stretch further and cooked on a hot griddle with ghee until golden, crisp on the outside and soft and flaky within. It is served with a small bowl of thin dhal curry (yellow lentil soup with turmeric and curry leaves) and sometimes fish or mutton curry. In Singapore, prata is consumed at any time of day and is a staple at Indian Muslim mamak stalls that are often open 24 hours. Per 100g: 292.0 kcal, 6.5g protein, 12.2g fat, 39.5g carbohydrates, 1.8g dietary fibre, 318.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Glycemic Index: 62 (Medium (56–69)). Glycemic Load per serving: 74.6. Source: University of Sydney GI Database — white flour paratha / flatbread; GI elevated by the refined wheat flour and pan-frying in ghee. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 1018mg per 2-piece serving (320g) — about 44% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; primarily from the dhal curry and prata salt content. Patients with hypertension should limit the dhal curry portion and avoid adding additional salt; prata is a moderate-sodium choice compared to other Singapore hawker breakfast options. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: High. Refined wheat flour prata has a high GI (~62) and a high glycaemic load (~75 per 2-piece serving); the high fat content from ghee slows but does not prevent a significant postprandial glucose rise. Diabetic patients should limit prata to 1 piece per meal and prefer the dhal as the protein accompaniment over sugar dipping; whole wheat prata where available will have a meaningfully lower GI. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Moderate. Moderate saturated fat from ghee; refined wheat flour is pro-inflammatory at high consumption; dhal provides folate and plant protein with anti-inflammatory potential. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit prata frequency; the dhal accompaniment provides beneficial plant protein and folate, but the refined wheat and ghee content of the prata itself is pro-inflammatory at regular consumption. Note: Values represent 2 pieces plain prata + dhal combined. Egg prata adds approximately 78 kcal and 6.5g protein per piece. Coin prata (small thin version) has lower calorie density. Margarine versions common at budget stalls will have trans fat content.

This is **Fish Head Curry (Singapore Indian style)**. Singapore Fish Head Curry is a uniquely Singaporean Indian invention — a rich, tangy, mildly spicy curry of a whole halved red snapper or grouper head simmered in a coconut milk-based curry with okra, brinjal, tomatoes, and curry leaves, seasoned with tamarind for sourness, turmeric, and a complex South Indian-style spice base. The fish head yields gelatinous, flavourful meat from the cheeks, collar, and eye socket, which are prized for their rich texture. Fish Head Curry is considered one of Singapore's signature dishes, credited to a Tamil Indian cook in the 1940s who adapted a South Indian fish curry for Chinese customers. Per 100g: 113.0 kcal, 12.5g protein, 5.5g fat, 4.5g carbohydrates, 1.5g dietary fibre, 518.0mg sodium. Standard serving size is approximately 100g. Hypertension — Concern level: Moderate. Sodium approximately 2072mg per serving (400g) — about 90% of the 2300mg daily recommended limit; primarily from the curry paste, tamarind seasoning, and fish seasoning. Patients with hypertension should avoid additional salt and limit the coconut curry broth; the fish itself is an excellent lean protein source and the vegetables add potassium that helps offset sodium. Type2 Diabetes — Concern level: Low. Very low carbohydrate content in the curry itself (~4.5g/100g); high-quality fish protein with negligible glycaemic impact; glycaemic impact comes entirely from any accompanying rice. Fish Head Curry without rice is an excellent protein source with minimal glycaemic impact for diabetic patients; pair with a small portion of brown rice or limit white rice to 150g for optimal blood glucose management. Rheumatoid Arthritis — Concern level: Low. Red snapper and grouper are rich in omega-3 fatty acids; turmeric and curry leaves provide anti-inflammatory compounds; tamarind provides antioxidants. Fish Head Curry is an excellent dish for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the omega-3 fatty acids from the fish head (especially the gelatinous portions) and the anti-inflammatory spice profile make this one of the best hawker choices for inflammatory conditions. Note: Vitamin D and vitamin B12 are notably high from the fish. Selenium content is elevated from the seafood. The gelatinous portions of the fish head (cheeks, collar) are rich in collagen but this is not captured in standard nutrient analysis. The coconut milk contributes medium-chain saturated fatty acids.
